name: MLJAR supervised (Automated Machine Learning with Explainability)

performance.notes: Time-controlled optimization; total_time_limit parameter controls execution

agent_readiness.notes: AutoML with built-in Explainability reports. from supervised.automl import AutoML. automl = AutoML(mode='Compete', total_time_limit=3600, explain_level=2). automl.fit(X_train, y_train). automl.predict(X_test). Modes: Explain (simple), Perform (fast), Compete (best accuracy). explain_level: 0-2. Generates HTML reports with feature importance, SHAP values, fairness analysis, decision tree visualization. Handles: classification, regression, multiclass. Ensemble: stacking and voting. Uses: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, RF, LinearModels, Neural Networks.

gotchas: Generates many files in output directory — disk usage can be significant
HTML reports require a browser to view (not programmatically accessible)
Less mature than AutoGluon or FLAML for production use
Compete mode is slow — many models trained with stacking
best_when: When you want AutoML with built-in interpretability/explainability reports
Business stakeholder presentations needing visual model reports
Tabular data competitions (Kaggle-style with Compete mode)
When SHAP and feature importance analysis is required alongside predictions
avoid_when: Large-scale production ML serving (use simpler frameworks)
When explainability reports are not needed (overhead is unjustified)
summary: mljar-supervised provides AutoML with automatic explainability reports. AutoML(mode='Compete', total_time_limit=3600).fit(X, y). Three modes: Explain (fast+interpretable), Perform (balanced), Compete (Kaggle-grade accuracy). Generates HTML reports with SHAP values, feature importance, fairness analysis. Uses XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost/RF/NN with stacking ensembles. MIT license. Best when you need AutoML + visual explanations for business stakeholders.
